title: When Growth Prices Out the People Who Keep a Town Alive
excerpt: Small towns do not become unaffordable all at once. They lose affordability through early signals: longer commutes, rising rents, unfilled jobs, and young residents quietly concluding that there is no room for them to remain.
author: Ting Luo
body: Spend a few days in a small Montana city and the appeal is easy to understand. The streets are human-scaled. Public spaces feel usable. A coffee shop worker may talk to you as though you already belong. The mountains are close, the pace is slower, and the civic texture still feels personal.
That is exactly why these places are vulnerable.

A town does not become unaffordable all at once. It happens first in smaller signals: a teacher commuting from another county; a barista leaving a job because rent rose faster than wages; a nurse unable to find a lease near the hospital; a young person quietly concluding that the place where they grew up has no room for them as an adult.
By the time these stories become visible as a “housing crisis,” local government is already late.

Across attractive small cities and mountain towns, especially in the American West, a familiar pattern is emerging. A place becomes desirable because of its landscape, safety, culture, universities, recreation or quality of life. New residents arrive, often with incomes or assets from outside the local economy. Some work remotely. Some retire. Some buy second homes. Some purchase property as an investment or short-term rental.
None of this is inherently wrong. Freedom of movement is one of the strengths of American life. People should be able to move toward places where they hope to build better lives.
But freedom to arrive can quietly weaken freedom to remain.

When high-mobility wealth enters a small housing market, it does not simply add people. It can reset prices. Local workers are then forced to compete for housing not only with neighbors earning local wages, but with buyers and renters whose purchasing power comes from somewhere else. The market still looks open, but the terms of participation have changed.

This creates an ethical problem as much as an economic one: Who gets to enjoy a place’s beauty, safety and public life, and who bears the cost of maintaining it? A town’s charm is not produced by scenery alone. It is sustained by teachers, nurses, repair workers, clerks, servers, librarians, sanitation workers, public employees and small-business staff. If those people can no longer live there, the town is not simply becoming expensive. It is becoming extractive.
That is why small cities need more than a housing plan. They need an early warning system.
Local officials should watch for displacement signals before the crisis becomes irreversible.

First, track the gap between rent growth and local wage growth. If rents are rising faster than the wages of teachers, nurses, municipal employees, service workers, public safety staff and entry-level professionals, the community is not merely becoming more expensive. It is beginning to price out the people who keep it functioning.

Second, track worker commute patterns. When essential workers increasingly live farther from the city they serve, the housing problem has already become an infrastructure, labor and resilience problem. When a restaurant worker has to drive 45 minutes to serve a town’s visitors, that is not a sign of prosperity. It is a warning that the local economy is becoming dependent on people it refuses to house.

Third, distinguish between homes used as primary residences and homes used as investment assets, second homes or short-term rentals. A housing unit is not serving the same civic function in all cases. A home occupied year-round by a local worker contributes differently to the community than a house that sits empty for much of the year or cycles through weekend visitors.

Fourth, ask employers what jobs they cannot fill because applicants cannot afford to live nearby. Housing is workforce infrastructure. If a city’s schools, hospitals, restaurants, local government and small businesses cannot recruit or retain workers because of housing costs, the city’s affordability problem has become an economic development problem.

Fifth, listen to the language of young residents. When young adults begin to speak of leaving as inevitable, the community is losing more than population. It is losing continuity, memory and future civic participation.
These signals should trigger action before displacement hardens into a new normal.

The goal is not to stop newcomers from arriving. A closed city is not a healthy city. The goal is to make sure growth does not become extraction. If outside wealth benefits from a town’s beauty, safety, culture and public life, it should also help sustain the conditions that make that town livable.

Cities have tools available: workforce housing requirements, community land trusts, deed-restricted homes, public land for affordable housing, short-term rental regulation, vacancy tracking, second-home fees where legally permissible, employer-assisted housing partnerships and housing trust funds supported by tourism or high-end development revenues.

But the most important shift is conceptual. Local governments must stop treating affordability as a problem that begins only when the poorest residents are in crisis. In small cities, affordability is also about whether ordinary workers can remain part of the place they serve.

A town has not succeeded simply because more people want to move there. It has succeeded only if the people who teach, repair, clean, care for, feed, protect and operate it can still afford to live there.
The right to arrive matters. But so does the right to remain. Small cities that learn to sense displacement early will be better able to welcome growth without losing themselves.
